sentences.text: Yes.
I was-- I was able to travel, being that I was buying food for-- for that German guy, Bergman.
He let me go to a neighboring town, which is called Przasnysz.
And there was still a Jewish bakery there open.
And I was able to get bread, as-- as many as I could, as much as I could, plus what I used to buy for him.
I used to always buy for myself too.
He let me do that.
So I was able to come once a week to a home to-- to the ghetto, to my family and bring them food.
And that was the only mainstay of-- of living, because of the food that I brought, because the food that they were given in the ghetto was very minimal.
But after a while, once we finished the highway work, they-- the Germans started to evacuate all the farmers from the area.
And they were going to put up a army camp in that area.
And naturally, they put up a camp also for us, but it was really a camp, like a slave labor camp.
It was all fenced in, with wires.
And we were in the same barracks, but things were different.
There were already people there, you know, who were head of the camp.
It was conducted like a regular slave labor camp.
And there were a lot of beating going on.
And we were putting up-- we were working on putting up those barracks for the army.
It was Nosarzewo.
It was Nosarzewo camp, because the area was called Nosarzewo.
And we were working there, putting up barracks with-- we were working with Polacks, together.
And after a while, they brought in 2,000 Russian prisoners a war.
About 150 people in that camp.
But after a while, you know, they brought in-- it was going already in-- into late 1940, '41.
They bought in Russian prisoners of war.
And they were barracked in-- nearby.
But a while later, maybe a month and a half later, we were all locked in into the barracks, and nobody was allowed to go-- there was a curfew.
And we heard shots, a lot of shots.
And the next morning, we saw the graves.
All the Russian soldiers were all shot.
And they were throwing them into graves.
In fact, we had to-- to finish covering the graves, the pits, like.
They were in pits.
And this camp became very bad, you know.
And they hung somebody, also, in the camp for committing some infraction.
It was not a very big crime.
And, being that we worked for a private company, the company where we built the barracks was also built by a private German company.
And I got very friendly with one of the head-- we call 'meister,' which is the foreman of the company.
I got very friendly with him.
And I knew they had another place in town, in Mlawa, by the railroad, where they used to do some work, too.
And some Jewish people used to work there, too.
But they used to let them go home every day to the ghetto.

So I pleaded with him, and I asked him-- I says to him that, you know, I have a mother and brothers and sisters that are in Mlawa, in ghetto, and I would like to go back to the other place there to work.
So after a lot of begging, he transferred me to the other place.
Of course, I told him the whole story, that I'm a carpenter, you know?
I did work carpenter work, not that I knew much about it, but I did work.
And I was there.
So being there was much better, because I was every night home with my family.
And I also was able to provide some food for them, because at the railroad station, they were bringing in a lot of different things, you know?
And we were actually stealing food.
Whenever they unloaded potatoes or-- but mainly potatoes.
So we used to bag some-- take in bags some potatoes.
Or we used to tie our pants on the bottom and putting inside our pants some potatoes, because they don't-- they didn't allow you to do-- to take anything.
So we were there 'til the end of 1941.
But then they decided to evacuate half of the ghetto.

And we were in that part, you know-- because actually the Jewish committee, which took care of all the transports, of course, according to the orders from the Germans, they will take first people who are not from the town, but they'll take all the oncoming people from different towns, because there were people there from different towns.
So they first sent-- naturally, they gave priority to their own people, from their own town.
And they shipped first the people from the other towns.
So naturally we were not from this town.
We were from another town, so we-- we were among the Jewish people who were shipped out.
And we were shipped to Miedzyrzec, which is in the Lublin area, near Biala Podlaska.
It was a 90% Jewish town.
And they put us all up in the synagogue.
But we knew that my brother, who was in the army-- he was taken prisoner of war, and they were taken back to Poland.
We heard that story later on, you know.
But we heard they put him on a march from that German camp, prisoner-of-war camp.
They were marching towards Lublin.
And on the way they shot-- there were 600 Jewish prisoners of war.
They shot 400, and 200 arrived in Biala Podlaska.
And they were working-- there was an airport there, and they were working at the airport.
I don't know exactly what kind of work they did.
But there was a camp, there, at the airport.
They did some work.
So we knew that my brother was there in that town.
So-- How did you find that out?
Pardon me?
Well, mail was still going.
And we got mail, you know, from people who remained in-- see, we to-- we didn't stay too long there, because my older brother after a few days went right back to Mlawa.

He smuggled back, because they was-- from there, to go back to Mlawa, we had-- we had to cross a river, because Germany divided Poland into the Third Reich and part of the-- of the Polish government, which is the Polish-- they call it Gubernia Polska, which is like a puppet state.
So my brother after a few days went right back.
But my mother and my two sisters and myself and my younger brother went to the neighboring town where my brother was.
And he provided for us a place to stay.
We stayed in somebody else's apartment.
We got a room in somebody else's apartment.
And we were there for about three or four weeks.
And my mother decided I should-- my two sisters and myself should go back to Mlawa, because my-- my older brother, which was there back already, wrote us a card that we can come back.
So I had to go back with my two sisters.
I was never afraid of anything, but we had a problem where we-- we traveled by--- by railroad from Biala Podlaska to Warsaw.
But in Praga, which is a little town-- not a little town, which is like a part of Warsaw, but it's called Praga.
So we had to-- we had to change trains there.
But the problem over there was, Polacks used to go around in the station and seek out Jewish people and then report them to the Germans.
So my older sister, who didn't look like Jewish at all-- but my younger sister was dark, and she looked Jewish.
But myself, I don't know I looked Jewish or not.
I don't know.
But I-- I was wearing like a big coat with a four-- with a four hat.
So it was like my face was buried in fur.
And I-- I put my head down, so they actually couldn't see much of me.
And-- but I-- I took my younger sister.
I like hid her in a spot there.
And I was like running around.
I didn't want to stay with them together, so there should be any suspicion.
Anyway, we-- the train came in, and we went back by train.
Then we came to the river.
And then, from the river, from there on, we had to smuggle the river.
We have to smuggle on the other side of the river.
So now we had to pay some Polacks to take us across.
It was winter already, and we can-- we walked on the ice.
It was dangerous, because some spots were already thin ice, but we-- we crossed the river.
And once we came across the river, we again were traveling by-- by railroad.
We had-- I had one case where we stopped in one of the towns and Germans came in, on the railroad.
And naturally they were looking for Jews.
So a German approached me.
And I start to cross myself, because I know how to do that, you know.
So I crossed myself, you know, like the-- the Christians do.
And you know-- and he said, in German, 'Verfluchte Pole,' you know?
And he let me go, and then he didn't bother my sisters anymore.
Well, anyway, we came into Mlawa, and we joined our brother.
Of course, he had the same-- the same room that we got.
And we stayed there [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for about seven, eight months.
And then my older brother was taken away and kept in a prison for about six days, five or six days.
Then we didn't hear anything, but people were saying that they were shipped to-- not only him.
There was a few people that were shipped to Auschwitz.
And we never heard from him since.
But then, in November 1942, they emptied the whole ghetto.
But first, they separated young people and older people so we were in separate wagons.
So we traveled [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a few days, maybe two days and a night, and we arrived in Auschwitz.
And right there, you know, they used to separate you, women separate, young men separate, old people separate, women with children separate.
So I was trying to get together with my sisters, but they wouldn't naturally-- I was hit in the head, and I was put back in with the young people.
And we marched from there, from the railroad station.
We marched to-- to the camp.
And my sisters were taken away and I didn't see where.
And all the other people were taken away also, to Birkenau, because the railroad station was in between Auschwitz and Birkenau.
We came to Birkenau.
They put us up in a big place.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then they took us into shower rooms.
They shaved us, took all our clothes away, naturally.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They put us up with kind of stinky liquid, you know.
It was disinfectant.
And then they tattooed our numbers.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were-- they were doing it alphabetically, so I-- I must have been-- because my name is K, so I got this number, because there were previous numbers and there were later numbers, because they were going up to the last letter-- to W, Z.
In fact, when they did the tattooing, it hurt, you know, because they go with needles.
At first, it's-- it's bloody.
Then it becomes blue, the way it is now.
But I just like twitched my head, and I was hit in the head by a hand.
But it was very hard, because I moved my head, you know.
After everything was done, they put us up into different blocks, different buildings.
We were put into 7A with a group of young people, all young people, which was the upper part of the building.
It was 7 and 7A.
So we were on the top, on the second floor.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The head of the block was a German of Polish descent, which was named Alfred Olshevsky.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he was screaming and yelling and kicking and beating.
And each one was assigned to a bed.
They were triple beds.
And they showed us where the washroom is.
And he told us, everybody has to be clean, has to wash himself, and he don't want anybody caught with dirty hands or dirty fingers or dirty feet or dirty ears or dirty head.
And then they put us-- the next morning, they put us up in a room on the top of the building.
It was like an attic.
And they taught us [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to be bricklayers.
First, they taught us about cement, about sand, about mixtures, about other things.

Then they showed us how do lie the bricks down, to use a scale, to use the-- you know, when you put up-- when you run a wall, you put up a string, you know, like, stretch a string from one end to the wall to the other so you-- the bricks lay straight.
We were there-- luckily, we were there for about two months.
It was a very cold winter.
And we were sitting there-- Now, the food was very minimal, no food.
The bread was like sawdust, because we saw, at the end, that the-- like, sawdust-- actually sawdust.
And we had a kapo there which was very, very nice.
But we had his helpers, who were very bad.
In fact, they were Jewish guys from France.
One was from France, was Maurice.
And one was from Slovenia.
And they behaved very bad.
They were kicking also.
And for any little infraction, they beat you, they kicked you.
And then in the-- in the-- every morning was roll call, and every evening was roll call.
They called your numbers, not your names.
And you had to answer to your number that you're there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [SIGH] after two months they put us up on construction.
But in-- in the interim-- I think I mentioned I was a big eater.
And I approached a man in camp which was already months before me there.
It was an older man.
He was not old, because I was young, so I considered him old.
He was my oldest brother's age.
And he was a carpenter.
I knew he worked outside the camp, Auschwitz camp.
And he had the possibility of acquiring a little more food.
So I approached him, and I asked him if he could help me with anything in food.
And he said he cannot, but if I know anybody who has gold coins or dollars or German marks, he will be able to help me with a loaf of bread or with a piece of salami.
Well, I immediately went into work and I start asking around.
And I said, whatever I'll get, we'll split.
Anyway, as-- when we came into camp, everything was taken.
All the clothes was taken away from us.
But the shoes, they let us take.
So some people, for some reason, had either gold coins or money in their heels, buried in their heels.
Well, one of the men, as a young man, he had-- I don't exactly remember, was it marks or dollars.
But I think it was dollars.
And I really didn't know dollars then.
But anyway, I took the dollars, and I brought it to him.
And the first night, he gave me a loaf of bread, which was a big thing.
So I shared.
I gave him half, and I took half.
And I took the other half-- my half I shared with another young man from my hometown, which was sleeping above me.
For some reason, people, because people were hungry, became very envious.
And somebody must have said something to somebody, and I was called out by the-- it's called the 'political abteilung.' It was the political section of the Gestapo.
In the evening, after roll call, my number was called out.
And I really didn't know why.
But I already got scared.
And I was called in to that political section of the Gestapo.
And they start asking me, who gave me the money and who did I give the money to?
Well, I already heard so many stories about what's going on in camp, so I figured, you know, I'm not going to involve anybody else anymore, that, you know, this is going to be my end, and that's it.
And I resigned to the idea that that's it.
So I wouldn't involve.
So they-- I got beat-- they were beating me.
They were hanging me on-- [CRYING] [DRINKS] They were hanging me by my hands, in the back, for about a half an hour, for 20 minutes.
And then they were kicking me with bayonets.
In fact, I have a big mark, here, [INAUDIBLE] stomach.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And after a few weeks, they let me go and I went back to work.
But my kapo told me that I have a death sentence, that whenever they're going to call me, in the evening, after roll call, that I am transferred to Block 11A.
So I knew that I'm being shot there, because there was a black wall there, he told me, and they put you up against the black wall and they shoot you.
This lasted like six months.
I don't know why.
But after six months, the head of the camp, of the Auschwitz camp, was, naturally, a German, and he was number one.
His name was Bruno.
He was in there for sabotage, because I know he-- I remember he had a-- a black triangle.
But his number was number 1.
He was caught, you know, with an SS woman in bed, you know.
And that's what the story was.
I-- I don't know.
That's what the story was.
And he was relieved of his job.
And in came in another German, with a red triangle, a political.
He had a number 62,000-- I don't remember-- the other numbers, I don't remember.
But I remember 62,000, because I saw him.
So my kapo came over to me, and he said to me-- and he knew that I had-- because he was the one who-- to tell me that I had a death sentence.
He told me there is a chance that my sentence could be taken out from the-- if it is in the camp chancellery, there's a possibility to take that sentence out.
If it's in the SS chancellery, then is no way.
So-- but he said to me but he needs a ladies-- a gold ladies' watch.
I didn't-- I had no means of getting anything like this.
But while we were working already on construction, we put up buildings for the Germans, storage houses like.
And over there I met people from Birkenau.
And I knew a cousin of mine was working in-- in the Sonderkommando, which was the crematorium, also a couple men from my hometown and a cousin of mine from a neighboring town.
So I said to a man from my hometown, also, named Braun, I said to him, if he can talk to them, I need-- and I told him the story, that I need a ladies' gold watch, if they can bring it to me.
Well, the next day he brought me a ladies' gold watch.
And I gave him-- to the kapo, my kapo-- I gave him the watch.
And, good enough, in the evening, he came back, and he brought me the death sentence.
And he said to me, you've got a new lease on life.
Not that we knew I'm going to survive, you know, that I knew I'm going to survive.
But at least I knew I'm back alive.
I was down to maybe, I don't know, 85, 90 pounds, because I didn't eat whatever they gave me.
So then, you know, you always hope.
And I worked.
I worked.
And then they transferred us.
Being that we worked in between Auschwitz and Birkenau, they transferred us to Birkenau.
I worked in a strictly Polish kommando.
It was a Polish group.
I was the only Jew there.

And I heard a lot of, you know, remarks about Jews, you know, because most of the guys working in the same kommando or the same group were criminals, because they also had-- they all had green triangles, which meant that they were in prison before the war, you know, in prison.
They were criminals.
A few of them were political, also.
But I was a very good worker.
And I slept in the barrack where they worked, so I didn't have to work on Sunday, because Jews had to work even Sundays.
They used to take the Jews out on Sunday, and they really didn't work.
It was they make them carry stones from one place and putting the stones back in the other place.
That's what they used to do on Sundays.
Otherwise, during the week they worked on different places.
So I went through several selections by Mengele.
And I was healthy.
But the last selection, which was in 1943, the end of 1943, was at night.
They gave an order, all Jews are out in the-- in the main street in the camp.
And we all formed-- you know, like, we stood in six-- which was a long line, because we were mostly Jews.
And they were-- they said they're selecting Jews to work in the crematorium.
But they did select some.
But most of them they-- they took to the gas chambers.
At the time of this last selection, I had like little [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] pus, you know, little pieces on my chest, all over my chest, here.
And I was afraid that if I approach him and he sees this, then I'm a real-- a sure candidate for the gas chambers.
So I moved back as far as I could.
Slowly I moved back the rows.
I moved to the last row.
And I ran.
They were chasing me, but I ran into one of the blocks where the Sonderkommando was still based in our camp.
And I was hiding there, and they couldn't find me.
And I-- lucky enough, they just left.
And then I went back to my-- to my block.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We-- I worked for that time for a company, a German company by the name of Neubau.
That was a construction company.
We used to do all kinds of work in the camps.
We used to fix the ovens.
They used to split from the heat, so we used to-- in the crematoriums.
We used to fix the ovens.
We used to fix toilets.
You know, there was public toilets in the camps, in the ladies' camps, in the women's camps.
And there was a quarantine camp, also.
And then, after a while, they brought Jews from Theresienstadt.
There were whole families, Czechoslovakian families.
But then they killed them all anyway.
So we used to go-- we had a-- like a paper that we could travel, not travel, but walk from camp to camp without any interruption.
And then-- they-- I meant for you to--
